does: This paper develops a pipeline for training automated red teaming models that can generalize across diverse adversarial objectives without requiring pre-existing evaluators. The authors finetune small language models like Qwen-3-8B using their proposed approach and demonstrate significant improvements in generating adversarial attacks for both in-distribution and out-of-distribution adversarial goals. Unlike prior work that targets specific safety domains through content moderation classifiers, this method trains a general-purpose red teamer capable of discovering vulnerabilities aligned with arbitrary attacker intents, expanding the scope of automated adversarial testing beyond traditional safety and content moderation concerns.
connects: This work extends prior research on automated red teaming of language models, which has primarily focused on circumventing safety and content moderation systems through adversarial prompts. Earlier methods relied on reward models or safety classifiers as evaluators to guide attack generation, limiting their applicability to predefined safety objectives. The paper builds on broader work in adversarial machine learning and jailbreaking, where discovering model vulnerabilities through iterative attack refinement has proven effective. It responds to the limitation that current red teaming approaches are narrowly scoped to content safety, connecting to more general adversarial evaluation frameworks that seek comprehensive vulnerability discovery across multiple threat models and objectives.
enables: This general-purpose red teaming capability opens pathways for more thorough and adaptable LLM evaluation beyond static benchmarks and predefined safety criteria. Future work can leverage such models to discover emergent vulnerabilities specific to individual model architectures or training procedures, supporting continuous adversarial testing as models evolve. The approach enables researchers to probe models for arbitrary adversarial intents—from misinformation to code generation exploits to reasoning failures—without needing task-specific evaluators. This generalization capability could support development of more robust LLMs by identifying previously unknown failure modes, and potentially inform the design of better defense mechanisms through systematic characterization of attack surfaces across diverse objectives.
